speaker: interviewer
text: Felix Pierson. August 12, 1996. We left off in Kaufering I. And you said you heard American guns.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. In the meantime, the Germans wanted us out. And my father said, my child, let's go. I said, I can't. I was grown to get hit. I had sores underneath my knees-- in my arms. I can't, I said. So my father said, no, I'm going to carry you. Had we stayed there and hour later, maybe half an hour later, the Americans were there. Meantime, as we moved out, my father carried me. I don't know where he got the strength. That Obersturmfuhrer Temple kicked me in my seat. And by the time I got to Dachau, it was past when I had to be operated. And by the way, that Temple was hung in Poland after the war. So when we came on the train, and we started to travel near the village-- Schaffhausen, something like that, a German military train stopped next to us. And the English or American planes-- I thought it was English. Now, I hear that there was Americans. And they were shooting at us. And they killed many of the inmates-- many of the Jewish people that were in [NON-ENGLISH]. I jumped up.  I threw myself off that plane.

speaker: interviewer
text: Train.

speaker: interviewee
text: That train. My father had a can of-- I don't know where he got it from-- from food. He held it in his hand. And somebody wanted to pull it out of his hand. He cut his hand. By the time we came-- it took three days at 60 kilometers. It took three days to get to Dachau. By the time we came to Dachau, my father had gangrene in his left hand. They amputated his arm, and it didn't help him. He died two days after the liberation in Dachau. I was operated, cleaned out-- the wound that I had. I don't remember how long I was there. And then I was transferred from there. Dachau was liberated by the American soldiers, by the American Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what the date was?

speaker: interviewee
text: April 30. I was in a haze at that time. Somebody said Kaddish with me. But I remember like in a haze because I myself was sick at the time. Then we were transferred to the [NON-ENGLISH] in Munich where we lived for a while. And from there, I found out that I had an aunt living in Landsberg. So I started out with-- I got a lift half of the way and 30 kilometers or 40 kilometers. We walked. I was with another man-- to my aunt. And I was lucky to find my aunt and two daughters survived. And they were living in Landsberg. And I stayed with them for a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your aunt's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My god-- Sarah Frant. Two daughters-- and we stayed with them for a while. In the meantime, I joined the DP police force. I was very much involved in the work that was done over there. I was in charge of the prison where we kept mostly people that collaborated with the Germans. Or the German police would send us Jewish people that they caught. For whatever reasons they had them. So we took them over. And they were not criminals to us. In Landsberg, I met my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your wife's full name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hermina. Her maiden name is Feher. F-E-H-E-R. And it was doing my duty as a policeman that I came to her apartment-- her room where she lived. And immediately, I said to myself, this is the kind of a girl that my parents would want me to marry. I was at that time 19 years old in 1945. Of course, we had this engagement where my school teacher was there [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with the rabbi in a short time would be-- because my wife had two sisters. But the older sister that was sort of a guardian wouldn't go back to Czechoslovakia until she knew that we were legally engaged. And in April 2, 1946, we got married. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We had a beautiful life. Now, it's 50 years that we have been married.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: In April 27, 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any child born in Landsberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our oldest son was born in Landsberg. April the 8th, 1949. In other words, when we left the house, he was 17 days. When he came to the United States, it was already 19 days.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Harry Michael.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so you arrived in New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: On April 27.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you came by--

speaker: interviewee
text: We came by plane, because there was a transport by the-- what was the organization? Not UNRRA-- it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: HIAS?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. It was International Refugee, IRO, International Refugee Organization. The children and pregnant women came on that plane. So we stayed for a while at my wife's relatives. But on the following-- I came on a Tuesday. And the following Monday, I was already working as an operator. I was quickly disillusioned with our Jewish brethren working in the factory. These were people that came 50 years before. And I didn't judge them. They were lucky enough to avoid the tragedy that we did. But they thought they were better than us. And they didn't treat us right. I had this manager in the factory. There was a Jewish immigrant that he made the statement one day that [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] which means the Germans should swell up-- should drop dead or whatever, because they didn't kill the refugees-- because maybe he had a problem with one. That was the attitude, while millions of American Jews did the best-- money-wise and other ways-- to help settle the refugees, there were individuals like that that must be remembered too.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I asked him, how could you say that? You come from Europe. You could have been there with us. He said, landsman, I don't mean you. You are the good one. I said, anti-Semites talk like that. Even the Germans, they found the good Jew. This guy, this gendarme, that  my father made the leather coats for him. He said, oh, Jews like you, I like.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, life goes on. And I quit that job. And I became a foreman. And then I had my own factory built on a shoestring where we employed 80 people and developed a good reputation.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had another son?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had another son, Norman Lee was born in Brooklyn the 9th of June, 1952. Our oldest son got a doctorate in mathematics. He was a professor in the University of Edmonton, Alberta. Our youngest son took over the business that I was involved, but not the factory. After I gave up the factory, I got involved in importing men's clothing from Poland, Hungary, Czechoslovakia, Bulgaria, Yugoslavia. And it was the best thing that I ever did that knowing my languages, I took advantage of that-- knowing the trade, and knowing how to deal with people. So after I retired, my youngest son took over the business. He's doing very well. We have six beautiful grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please name them.

speaker: interviewee
text: They are all geniuses-- Robert, that was in January 17. Than I have our darling Elizabeth, the finest granddaughter and daughter anyone wants to have-- Elizabeth Ann. Then we have Jonathan David-- fine, good-looking, bright young man. And then we have Matthew Eric, our youngest son, oldest, that gets the highest marks. And all these children are the joy of our lives. Then we have Daniel Noah. That's our oldest son, the youngest. He is 10 years old. And he is going on 20. He is very, very bright. And our sweet, youngest granddaughter Jennifer Rose that is a little darling. She said to me the other day, Grandpa, you're going to always love me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just before we leave them, tell me the full name of Harry's wife. OK, and Norman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Norman's wife is Joanne.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Fine. Just to have everyone in the family.

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest son is divorced from his wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very important, you have gone back to visit Poland, I think recently. Can you tell us about those,  please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was in Poland in 1945. [NON-ENGLISH] was still the place where charred bones were laying all over. And I was able to pick up some charred bones-- send parts to Israel with our Rabbi Eisenstein. I think his name was-- I can't remember the first name. Eisenstein-- he took some of these charred bones to Israel where he put up our monument. And some of the bones we have in Beth David cemetery in Long Island where we meet every year. And we say Kaddish. First, this is organized by the Lasker Society of which I was president for a number of years. And we meet there every year. And we reminisce. And we say Kaddish. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Of course, I would be amiss not mentioning how wonderful the United States of America has been to us. I think that of all the countries in the world except Israel, there's no place that I would be able to accomplish-- or any of us, or any Jewish people. Some might not realize, especially those born in this country, that this is the sweetest diaspora that was possible for the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a place where it's more important that America be born in you than you in America, because America stands for an ideal. We have a Constitution that protects everyone. And it's a very wonderful place to be. And I only have only one regret, that I wasn't born here and that I didn't come here to come here with my parents, because I missed that here, and I should have mentioned it as we started the interview. While  our children were-- Harry came here as a child-- somehow they suffer as survivors. They don't have a holiday with the grandparents. And the holidays were the worst-- the worst of any time of the year. There were times when children want to know, how come we don't have any grandparents? Any uncles? Any aunts? Why? Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: I personally am very active. And I'm on the board of the synagogue-- on board of the community center where I live now in Lake Louise Marie was president. And the one time at a speech that I gave, I said this is a celebration to be able to live in America. To live in Lake Louise is wonderful. But it's a Jewish custom at every celebration to remember those that weren't able to attend the celebration. And our dear pious, innocent parents that never harmed a soul in the world-- my brother of 12 years old. My father-- the beautiful songs he used to sing [NON-ENGLISH] after a meal. But his biggest wish was to be able to have a [NON-ENGLISH] three grown mature people, bar mitzvah people. He didn't live to that. And my father did so much good in this world. And there was no reason in the world that this man should be cut down at that young age. And my mother died-- died, she didn't die. She was murdered at the age of 37. This woman didn't hurt a fly in the world. And now we have people, Jewish people born in this country, that are bored with the Holocaust-- too much. They heard it too much. It's boring for them. And they compared it to all the music that is boring. We have non-Jews that say it never happened. Well, maybe they can't comprehend what it means 6 million. Maybe they would understand if it would happen to their families at least some, because I remember my father saying, my child, if we survive, there won't be any Hanukkah. There won't be any Pesach. The world will celebrate our coming out of slavery, because such cruel slavery there was never in history. But the world goes on. You have slavery now. You have cruelties now. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm 70 years old now. I miss my parents, badly. My wife misses her parents. I have a wonderful wife of 50 years. We have grown up together. But from time to time, we look at each other. And how would it be if our families would be able to enjoy our children, our grandchildren? Why not? What did they do? What wrong did they do in this world that that happened to them? All the monuments in the world can't compensate for that. But it is. It happened. And I myself sometimes wonder did that really happen? I think we will close with that important note. And now your family, your grandchildren, will have your memories and memories of your parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just hope they have a more peaceful world where things like that don't happen to them, because they are all fine people. And this should be a lesson to the whole world because it happened today to Jews. It can happen tomorrow to what is like Pastor Niemoller had said. He didn't help in time. And then there was no one around to help him.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just hope that our grandchildren and our children and our grandchildren will have a more peaceful world and more peaceful life than we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: I agree with you. Thank you very much for giving us this time and all your memories.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. This is a picture of my mother Rochma Piernikarz that I have received from Argentina after the war. That picture was probably sent to Argentina when my mother was 18 years old. That's the only picture that I have of my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of the [NON-ENGLISH], which means a credit union that loaned money with interest to the people in our town of Lask. On the right, there is a square. My father is on the right of that picture. That is the only picture I have of my father. And I received this from Belgium. I took this part from my father and the previous picture. And I made a portrait. And that's the only picture that I have of my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: All these people lived in Lask.

speaker: interviewee
text: All these people, the respected citizens, the board of directors from the town. In the middle, the one with without a hat sitting. This was the doctor, Dr. Felcher, Dr. Singer, that survived the war because he was hidden by the Poles. They hid him out because he was considered one of them. He was a very [INAUDIBLE] person. And standing in back of him is Yosef Blanket. Sitting next to Felcher is Mortafavel Kochman. He had the brewery in town. The second from the right side is Zitmanovitch. He had the store of trimmings for clothing. One of them is Hanoch Zendal. I don't know which one. I couldn't remember. These were all respected citizens-- all better financially situated people than the rest of the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of me taken in 1945 by the end of the year when I was visiting Poland-- not really visiting. I went to bring back my aunt to Germany. And this picture was taken. This picture was taken in Lask in 1945. That was the end of the year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of me and my wife Hermina taken in Landsberg in the year 1947. At that time, I was in the DP police force and the rank of a sergeant. I must note that those boots that I am wearing, my wife had paid for. That was a present from my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your wife's full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hermina Feher. That's her maiden name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is an ID card that belonging to the DP police force. Before we were able to get this ID card like that, I had to attend an American military police school in Ravensburg. And after I got the diploma, I was able to become a sergeant in the displaced persons police in the Landsberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Landsberg, Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Landsberg am Lech, Germany where Hitler was imprisoned when he made the putsch and where he wrote

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of me and my beautiful wife Hermina, who has been a partner in my life for over 50 years now and has given me a beautiful family to live and to enjoy every day as we are together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was taken in 1993? Where was it taken? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: It might be 1993. We were at the synagogue luncheon. Here in Lake Louise Marie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here on this picture I'm standing in front of monument that is symbolizing the uprising of the Warsaw Ghetto that occurred on April 1943. This picture was taken in Warsaw in the year 1978.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's you standing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm standing in front of that picture at this monument. And that moment was designed by Rapoport. And it's a tremendous, big monument where every year on the anniversary of the uprising, people bring flowers and say prayers. And in 1983, at the 40th anniversary of ghetto uprising, I was there with hundreds of Jewish people from all over the world where we honored the martyrs of the uprising of the Warsaw Ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of our entire family-- our treasure-- our joy. This is what we have accomplished earlier in the United States by creating such a beautiful family after such a terrible tragedy. The picture was taken on our 50th anniversary where we took our dear family on a cruise in the Caribbean. And it was a peaceful cruise where we enjoyed for the first time really to have everyone together and enjoying their company.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please start at the bottom left with your grandson that's sitting and tell us each name.

speaker: interviewee
text: To the left is Jonathan David. Next to him, is our daughter-in-law, which is really our daughter Joanne. Then there is our darling little Jennifer. Then is my wife, Hermina. Sitting next to my wife is Elizabeth. And going the other way is Danny-- 10 years old. In back of him is his father Harry, our son Harry. Next to him is I am standing. That is Matthew Eric, our youngest son, oldest. That is our youngest son, Norman. And Robert has turned 17 on January 1996.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.
